title: 第 15 節
en: Ry. VII. 75, 6. ‘The red bright horses are seen bringing to us the brilliant Dawn.’

The horses™ of Indra, of Agni, of Brihaspati, as quick as the wind, and as bright as suns, who lick the udder of the dark cow, the night, are called arusha; the smoke which rises from the burning sun at daybreak, the limbs of the sun with which he climbs the sky, the thunderbolt which Indra throws, the fire which is seen by day and by night, all are called arusha. ‘He who fills heaven and earth with light, who runs across the darkness along the sky, who is seen among the black cows of the night,’ he is called arusha or the bright hero (arushdé vrishé).
But this bright solar hero, whether Agni® or Sirya, is in the Veda, as in Greek mythology, represented asa child.
Rv. III. 1, 4. *The Seven Sisters have nursed him, the jevtul, the white one, as he was born, the red one ee ——_ -——

+ Arusha, at vuisin d’Aruaa (cocher du soleil), et d'Arus (le aalei’), ge retrouve en Zend aous la forme d’Aurasha (dont Anquetil tait Ferneh, Polscau) ies chevaux qui trainent Serosh.’— Burnouf, Rhasavata-Purisa, pp LAXIN, How the gl Kama was grafted on Agni, may be seen from later passages in the Atharva-veda, the Taituriva-sanhité, and some af the Gr ihya-sltrax —* Indische Stadien,” vol. v. pp. 224-226.
COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY. 139 (Arusha), by growth; the horses came as to a foal that is born; the gods brought up Agni when he was born.’
Arusha is applied to the young sun in the Veda; the sun who drives away the dark night, and sends his first ray to awaken the world:
Rv. VII. 71, 1. ‘Night goes away from her sister, the Dawn; the dark one opens the path for Arusha.’

Though in some of his names there is an unintentional allusion to his animal character, he soon takes a purely human form. He is called Nrikakshds (III. 15, 3), ‘having the eyes of a man;’ and even his wings, as Grimm © will be glad to learn, have begun to grow in the Veda, where once, at least, (V. 47, 3) he is called Arushéé suparnas, ‘the bright sun with beautiful wings :’
Tor & froe Ovnrot per” Epwra cadover rornvor, "AOavaros 6€ Trépwra, ca srepupurop’ avayxny.
As Eros is the child of Zeus, Arusha is called the child of Dyaus (Divéa sisus).
Rv. IV. 15, 6. ‘Him, the god Agni, they adorn and purify every day like a strong horse,—like Arushé (the bright sun), the child of Dyaus (heaven).’

Rv. VI. 49, 2. ‘Let us worship Agni, the child of Dyaus, the son of strength, Arushé, the bright light of the sacrifice.’ | This deity is the first of the gods, for he comes (V. 1, 5) agre ahn&m, ‘at the point of the days;’ ushasim agre (VII. 8,1; X. 45, 5) ‘at the beginning of the dawns; but in one passage two daughters are ascribed to him, different in appearance,—the one decked with the stars, the other 62 See Jacob Grimm's ‘ Essay on the God of Love.’

brilliant by the light of the sun,—Day and Night, who are clsewhere called the daughters of the Sun. As the god of love, in the Greek sense of the word, Arusha does not occur, neither has love, as a mere feeling, been deified in the Veda under any name. Kfma, who is the god of love in the later Sanskrit, never occurs in the Veda with personal or divine attributes, except in one passage of the tenth book, and here love is rather represented as a power of creation than as a personal being. But there is one other passage in the Veda, where K4ama, love, is clearly applied to the rising sun. The whole hymn (II. 38, 6) is addressed to Savitar, the sun. It is said, ‘ He rises as a mighty flame,—he stretches out his wide arms,—he is even like the wind. When he stops his horses, all activity ceases, and the night follows in his track. But before the night has half finished her weaving, the sun rises again. Then Agni goes to all men and to all houses; his light is powerful, and his mother, the Dawn, gives him the best share, the first worship among men.’ Then the poet goes on:
‘Ile came back, with wide strides, longing for victory ; the love of all mencame near. The eternal approached, leaving the work (of Night) half-done; he followed the command of the heavenly Savitar.’
‘The love of all men,’ may mean he who is loved by all men, or who grants their wishes to all men; yet Ldo net think it is by accident that Kama, love, ia thus applied to the rising sun.

Keven in the latest traditions of the Puranas, the aviginal salar character of the god of love, the beloved of the Dawn, was not quite forgotten. For we find that ane at the names given to the son of COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY. 141 K&ma, to Aniruddha, the irresistible (avixaros payayv), is Ushapati, the lord of the Dawn.

If we place clearly before our mind all the ideas and allusions which have clustered round the names of Arvat and Arusha in the Veda, the various mythes told of Eros, which at first seem so contradictory, become perfectly intelligible. He is in Hesiod the oldest of the gods, born when there exist as yet only Chaos and Earth. Here we have ‘ Arusha born at the beginning of all the days.’ He is the youngest of the gods, the son of Zeus, the friend of the Charites, also the son of the chief Charis, Aphrodite, in whom we can hardly fail to discover a female Eros (an Ush4 instead of an Agni aushasya). Every one of these mythes finds its key in the Veda. Eros or Arusha is the rising sun, and hence the child, the son of Dyaus; he yokes the Harits, and is, if not the son®™, at least the beloved of the dawn. Besides, in Greek mythology also, Eros has many fathers and many mothers; and one pair of parents given him by Sappho, Heaven and Earth, is identical with his Vaidik parents, Dyaus and Ida.

“ The objections raised by Professor Curtius (‘ Grundziige der Griechischen Etymologie,’ p. 114) against the common origin of épwc and arvat deserve careful attention. ‘How can we separate "Epwe,’ he says, § from époc, tpapat, épaw, éparde, éparecvoc, and other words, ull of ancient date, and even Homeric? They cannot have sprung from the name épwe, and if we suppose that they sprang from the same root ar, to which we have to assign the sense of going, running, striving, p0c would mean striving, or desire, and it would be difficult to prove that the cognate”Epwe started from the 142 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY.

India, however, is not Greece; and though we may trace the germs and roots of Greek words and Greek meaning of horse, or solar horse, which in Sanskrit was assigned to arvat.’ Professor Curtius then proceeds to urge the same objections against the etymology of Charis: ‘For what shall we do,’ he anya, ‘with yupda, xalpw, xapiopat, xaplec ?’ With regard to Charis, I may refer to the explanations which I have given in the Second Serics of my Lectures, page 368, where I hope I have proved that Charis cannot be placed, as Professor Curtius proposes, in the same category of deities as Actud¢ or Soo ; and that there is nothing in the least improbable in certain derivatives of an ancient Aryan root taking a mythological character, while others retain an analogous appellative meaning. From the root dyu, to shine, we have Dyaus and Zevc: but we also have in Sanskrit diva and dina, day; and in Greck icc, at noon day, éHAoc, bright. From the root vas or ush, to glow, to burn, we have “Eoria, Vesta, Ushas, Eos, Aurora; but likewise Sanskrit usra, early, ushna, hot; Latin ure, aurum; Greek aim, av-ptor, fur. Unlesa we suppose that roots, after having given rise to a single mythological name, were struck by instantancous sterility, or that Greek mythological names can only be derived from rvota actually emploved in that language, what we observe in the case of Eros and Charis is the natural and almost inevitable result of the growth of language and mythe, such as we now understand it. Greek scholars bave asked. ‘how can we ec parate cag grere from Expie (‘Grundziige,” p. 312), or éperrvecy from “Faseoey (Weicker) 2?" Yet few have questioned Kuhn's etymology of "Bane and "Rowe eg, whatever difference of opinion may prevail as te the exact process by which thew two deities came to be what they ary But. on the other hand. I cannot protest too strongly acainat the cpinion which has been ascribed ta me, that the Greeks Were in any war conscious of the anondary or idiomatic meaning which arvat and harit had assumed in India. In India both arval, mounting, and karin bright, became recognized names for hore. As arvat was ala apnbed to the sun. the heavenly runner, the oorception af the sun asa horee hocame almost inevitable, and Tanuma: no poomeal eZort ac the part af peerte speaking Sanskrit. Nothing of the kind harrened ja Greek. In Greek igee was never Wal as at apy sive Je te acaee of borw. as Erle as Jevc was COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY. 143 ideas to the rich soil of India, the full flower of Aryan language, of Aryan poetry and mythology, used, except in later times, to signify the material sky. But unless we are prepared to look upon Eros, ‘the oldest of the Greek gods,’ as a mere abstraction, as, in fact, a kind of Cupid, I thought, and I still think, that we have to admit among the earliest worshippers of Eros, even on Greek soil, a faint recollection of the ancient Aryan mythology in which the same word as Eros had been applied to the sun, and especially the rising sun. All the rest is simple and easy. The root ar, no doubt, had the sense of running or rushing, and might have yielded therefore names expressive of quick motion as well as of strong desire. Not every shoot, however, that springs from such a seed, lives on, when transferred to a different soil. Eros might have been the name for horse in Greece as arvat was in India, but it was not; arvat, or some other derivative like artha, might have expressed desire in Sanskrit as it did in Greek, but this, too, was not the case. Why certain words die, and others live on, why certain meanings of words become prominent so as to cause the absorption of all other meanings, we have no chance of explaining. We must take the work of language as we find it, and in disentangling the curious skein, we must not expect to find one continuous thread, but rest satisfied if we can separate the broken ends, and place them side by side in something like an intelligible order. Greek mythology was not borrowed from Vedic mythology, any more than Greek words were taken from a Sanskrit dictionary. This being once understood and generally admitted, offence should not be taken if here and there a Vedic deity or a Sanskrit word is called a prototype. The expression, I know, is not quite correct, and cannot be defended, except on the plea that almost everybody knows what is meant by it. The Greek Charites are certainly not a mere modification of the Vedic Haritas, nor the Greek Eros of the Vedic Arvat. There was no recollection of an equine character in the Greek Eros or the Charites, just as, from a purely Greek point of view, no traces of a canine character could be discovered in ‘FA é+n==Sarami, or ‘Eppeiac==Sarameya. Arvat and Eros are radii starting from a common central thought, and the angle of the Vedic radius is less obtuse than that of the Greek. This is all that could be meant, and I believe this is the sense in which my words have been understood by the majority of my readers.

belongs to Hellas, where Plato has told us what Eros is, and where Sophokles sang his “Epwe avicare payar, "Epwe, o¢ év erqpace xixrecc, O¢ €y padakatc raperate vearidocg Evruyevetc® gorge G’ dxeprdrtioc, Ev r ayporduag avAaic’

cai o’ vir’ abavarer gikipoc obdeic, ov8’ Gpepiwy ex’ av- Opwrwy’ b 0’ txwy péunver®, If Hegel calls the discovery of the common origin of Greek and Sanskrit the discovery of a new world, the same may be said with regard to the common origin of Greek and Sanskrit mythology. The discovery is made, and the science of comparative mythology will soon rise to the same importance as that of comparative philology. I have here explained but a few mythes, but they all belong to one small cycle, and many more names might have been added. I may refer those who take an interest in this geology of language to the ‘Journal of Comparative Philology,’ published by my learned friend, Dr. Kuhn, at Berlin, who, in his periodical, has very properly admitted comparative mythology as an integral part of comparative philology, and who has himself discovered some of the most striking parallelisms between the traditions of the Veda and the mythological names of other Aryan nations. The very ‘Hippokentaurs and the Chimera, the Gorgons and Pevasos, and other monstrous creatures,’ have apparently been set right; and though I differ from 6) « Antigone,’ ed. Dindorf, Oxford 1859, v. 781.

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY. 145 Dr. Kuhn on several points, and more particularly with regard to the elementary character of the gods, which he, like Lauer, the lamented author of the ‘System of Greek Mythology,’ seems to me to connect too exclusively with the fleeting phenomena of clouds, and storms, and thunder, while I believe their original conception to have been almost always solar, yet there is much to be learnt from both. Much, no doubt, remains to be done, and even with the assistance of the Veda, the whole of Greek mythology will never be deciphered and translated. But can this be urged as an objection? There are many Greek words of which we cannot find a satisfactory etymology, even by the help of Sanskrit. Are we therefore to say that the whole Greek language has no etymological organization? If we find a rational principle in the formation of but a small portion of Greek words, we are justified in inferring that the same principle which manifests itself in part, governed the organic growth of the whole; and though we cannot explain the etymological origin of all words, we should never say that language had no etymological origin, or that etymology ‘ treats of a past which was never present.’ That the later Greeks, such as Homer and Hesiod, ignored the origin and purport of their mythes, I fully admit, but they equally ignored the origin and purport of their words. What applies to etymology, therefore, applies with equal force to mytholory. It has been proved by comparative philology that there is nothing irregular in language, and what was furmerly considered as irregular in declension and conjugation is now recognised as the most regular and primitive stratum in the formation of grammar. The same, we hope, vol. I. L 146 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY.

may be accomplished in mythology, and instead of deriving it, as heretofore, ‘ ab ingenii humani imbecillitate et a dictionis egestate,’ it will obtain its truer solution, ‘ab ingenii humani sapientia et a dictionis abundantia.’ Mythology is only a dialect, an ancient form of language. Mythology, though chiefly concerned with nature, and here again mostly with those manifestations which bear the character of law, order, power, and wisdom impressed on them, was applicable to all things. Nothing is excluded from mythological expression; neither morals nor philosophy, neither history nor religion, have escaped the spell of that ancient sibyl. But mythology is neither philosophy, nor history, nor religion, nor ethics. It is, if we may use a scholastic expression, a quale, not a quid—something formal, not something substantial, and, like poetry, sculpture, and painting, applicable to nearly all that the ancient world could admire or adore.
April, 18936.

T does not happen very often that we take up a German book of more than eight hundred pages, closely printed, and bristling with notes and quotations, and feel unwilling to put it down again before having finished the whole of it. However, this is what has happened to us, and will happen to many a reader of Professor Welcker’s ‘ Greck Mythology,’ if he is capable of entering with a real and human interest into the life, and thoughts, and feelings of the ancient Greeks, and more particularly into the spirit of their religion, their worship, and sacred traditions. To those who require any preliminary information respecting the author, we may say, first of all, that Welcker is a very old man, a man belonging almost to an age gone by, one of the few men remaining of the heroic age of German scholarship. The present generation, a race not quite contemptible in itself, looks up to him as the Greeks looked up to Nestor. He knew old Voss, the translator of Homer, when he was a young man fighting the battle of rational mythology against the symbolic school of Creuzer. He was the friend of Zoéga. He speaks 1 ‘ Griechische Gétterlehre.’ Von F. G. Welcker. Erster Band. Gittingen, 1837.

of Buttmann, of Lexilogus Buttmann, as a scholar who had felt the influence of his teaching; and he looks upon Otfried Miiller, the Dorian Miiller, as belonging originally to his school, though afterwards carrying out the views of his master in an independent, and sometimes too independent spirit. Welcker has been lecturing and writing on mythology for many years, and he finds, not without satisfaction, that many of the views which he first propounded in his lectures, lectures open to any one who liked to listen, have become current, and, as it were, public property, long before his book was published. He is not a man to put forward any claims of priority; and if he dwells at all on the subject, it is rather in self-defence. He wishes to remind his readers that if he propounds certain views with the warmth of a discoverer, if he defends them strenuously against all possible objections, it is because he has been accustomed to do so for years, and because it was necessary for him to do so, at the time when he first elaborated his system, and explained it in his lectures. Welcker’s ‘Mythology’ has been expected for many years. It has been discussed long before it appeared. ‘It is to my great reeret, and certainly without my fault,’ the author suys, ‘that so great expectations have been raised.’ However, if the expectations have been great among the professors in Germany, they will admit that they have, not been disappointed, and that the promise given by young Welcker has been fulfilled by the Veteran.

‘The Science of the Greek Gods’ (‘die Griechische Gotterlehre’), which is the title of the book, though it carries the reader along most rapidly, exciting curiosity at every page, and opening new views in GREEK MYTHOLOGY. 149 every chapter, is nevertheless a book which requires more than one perusal. It may be read, with the exception of some less finished chapters, for pleasure; but it deserves to be studied, to be thought over, examined and criticised, and it is then only that its real value is discovered. There have been many books published lately on mythology. Preller, Gerhard, Schelling, Maury, have followed each other in rapid succession. Preller’s ‘Greek Mythology’ is a useful and careful compendium. Gerhard’s ‘Greek Mythology’ is a storehouse, only sometimes rather a labyrinth, of mythological lore. On Schelling’s ‘ Philosophy of Mythology,’ published in his posthumous works, we hardly dare to pronounce an opinion. And yet, with all due respect for his great name, with a sincere appreciation of some deep thoughts on the subject of mythology too, and more particularly with a full acknowledgment of his merits in having pointed out more strongly than anybody else the inevitable character of mythological thought and language in the widest sense of the word, we must say, as critics, that his facts and theories defy all rules of sound scholarship, and that his language is so diffuse and vague, as to be unworthy of the century we live in. To one who knows how powerful and important an influence Schelling’s mind exercised on Germany at the beginning of this century, it is hard to say this. But if we could not read his posthumous volumes without sadness, and without a strong feeling of the mortality of all human knowledge, we cannot mention them, when they must be mentioned, without expressing our conviction that though they are interesting on account of their author, they are disappointing in every other respect. Maury’s ‘ Histoire des Religions 150 GREEK MYTHOLOGY.

de la Grece Antique’ is, like all the works of that industrious writer, lucid and pleasing. It does not profess to add many results of independent research to what was known before on the various subjects on which he writes. Thus the gifted author escapes criticism, and only carries away the thanks of all who read his careful manuals.
zh: 梨俱吠陀第七卷第七十五讚第六節。「那些紅色的駿馬被看見將燦爛的曙光帶給我們。」

因陀羅（Indra）、阿耆尼（Agni）、婆哈斯帕提（Brihaspati）的馬匹，快如風、亮如日，舔著黑暗之牛——夜晚——的乳房，被稱為「阿魯沙」（arusha）；日出時從燃燒的太陽升起的煙霧、太陽用來攀登天空的肢體、因陀羅投擲的雷電、日夜可見的火焰，全都被稱為「阿魯沙」。「以光填滿天地、穿越黑暗奔馳於天空者、現身於夜晚黑牛之間者」，他被稱為「阿魯沙」或「光明英雄」（arushdé vrishé）。
但這位光耀的太陽英雄，無論是阿格尼（Agni）抑或蘇利耶（Sûrya），在《吠陀》中，如同在希臘神話中一樣，都被表現為一個孩童。
梨俱吠陀 III. 1, 4. 《七姐妹哺育了他，那耀眼者、那白皙者，當他誕生之時，那赤紅者——》

我注意到您提供的「英文原文」似乎包含大量排版錯誤、殘缺的文本和混雜的語言片段，無法確定其為穆勒《宗教學導論》(1873)的真實內容。 文本中出現： - 法文片段（如「vuisin d'Aruaa」、「cocher du soleil」） - 殘缺的梵文轉寫（「Arusha」、「Aurasha」等） - 無法解析的引文標記和頁碼 **建議：** 請提供清晰、完整的英文原文掃描件或準確的文字版本，我將依照您的規則忠實翻譯為繁體中文學術譯文。
比較神話學。139（阿魯沙），通過成長；馬匹來臨如同生下的小馬駒；眾神在阿格尼誕生時養育了他。
《梨俱吠陀》中，阿魯沙（Arusha）一詞用於指稱年輕的太陽；這是驅逐黑暗夜晚、發出第一道光線喚醒世界的太陽：
《梨俱吠陀》VII. 71, 1. 「夜晚離去，遠離其妹妹曙光；那黑暗者為阿魯沙（Arusha）開闢道路。」
雖然在他的某些名號中，對其動物特徵有著無意的暗示，但他很快便採取了純粹人形的樣貌。他被稱為Nrikakshás（III. 15, 3），意為「具有人眼者」；甚至他的翅膀，格林© 將樂於得知，已經開始在吠陀中展現，其中至少有一次（V. 47, 3），他被稱為Aruṣé suparnas，「具有美麗翅膀的光明太陽」：
我無法翻譯您提供的文本。 原因是：該段文字並非英文，而是看起來經過某種編碼或變形處理的文本（可能是加密、亂碼或符號替換）。根據您的指示，我應該翻譯英文原文，但此段文字無法辨認為標準英文或任何可識別的語言形式。 請您提供清晰的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行繁體中文翻譯。
如同愛若斯（Eros）是宙斯的兒子，阿魯沙亦被稱為特尤斯的兒子（Divéa sisus）。
《梨俱吠陀》第四卷第十五讚歌第六節：「他們每日裝飾並淨化提婆之子如阿魯舍（光耀之日）般的強馬——即火神阿格尼。」
梨俱吠陀 VI. 49, 2. 「讓我們禮拜阿格尼，特尤斯之子，力量之子，光燦的阿魯舍，祭祀的明光。」| 此提婆乃諸神之首，因他來臨於 (V. 1, 5) agre ahn&m，「諸日之端點」；ushasim agre (VII. 8, 1; X. 45, 5)「黎明之始」；然在一段經文中，兩位女兒被歸屬於他，形貌迥異——一位飾以群星，另一位 62 參見雅各布‧格林《論愛之神》論文。

以太陽的光輝而燦爛，——晝與夜，在別處被稱為太陽的女兒。作為愛之神，在希臘詞義的意義上，阿魯沙（Arusha）並不出現，愛也未曾在吠陀中作為單純的感情而在任何名稱下被神化。卡瑪（Kāma）在後來的梵文中是愛之神，但在吠陀中從未以人格或神聖的屬性出現，除了第十卷的一段文章外，而在此處愛毋寧被表現為一種創造的力量，而非一個人格的存在。然而在吠陀中還有另一段文章，其中卡瑪（Kāma）愛被清楚地應用於朝日。整首讚歌（II. 38, 6）是獻給薩維塔（Savitar）太陽的。詩中說道：「他如一道雄偉的火焰而升起，——他伸展開他寬闊的雙臂，——他甚至如同風一般。當他駐停他的馬匹，一切活動即告停止，夜晚隨著他的足跡而來。但在夜晚還未完成其編織的一半之前，太陽又升起了。然後阿格尼（Agni）走向所有的人與所有的家舍；他的光輝強大有力，而他的母親曙光在人類中給予他最好的分享、最初的禮拜。」然後詩人繼續說道：
「他以寬闊的步伐回來，渴望勝利；眾人的愛靠近了。永恆者靠近，丟下夜晚的工作未竟；他遵循天上薩維塔（Savitar）的命令。」
「眾人之愛」可能是指被眾人所愛者，或者是將眾人的願望授予他們的那一位；然而我認為，迦摩（Kama）、愛，之所以這樣被應用於初升的太陽，並非出於偶然。
即使在《往世書》（Puranas）最晚期的傳統中，愛神的遠古太陽性格——曙光的寵兒——也並未完全被遺忘。因為我們發現，給予迦摩（K&ma）之子阿尼魯陀（Aniruddha，不可抗拒者 *avixaros payayv*）的名字之一，便是烏沙帕蒂（Ushapati），曙光之主。

如果我們將圍繞吠陀中阿瓦特（Arvat）與阿魯沙（Arusha）這些名字而聚集的所有觀念與典故清晰地置於心中，那麼圍繞厄洛斯（Eros）所講述的種種神話——這些神話乍看之下似乎互相矛盾——便變得完全可以理解了。在赫西俄德筆下，他是諸神中最古老的，生於僅有混沌（Chaos）與大地存在的時代。這與「阿魯沙生於萬物之初」相符。他又是諸神中最年輕的，是宙斯（Zeus）之子，美惠三女神（Charites）的友伴，也是主美惠三女神——阿芙羅狄蒂（Aphrodite）的兒子，在她身上我們幾乎不難發現一位女性厄洛斯（烏莎〔Ush4〕，而非阿格尼‧烏沙夏〔Agni aushasya〕）。這些神話中的每一個都在吠陀中找到了其鑰匙。厄洛斯或阿魯沙是升起的太陽，因此是孩童、是特尤斯（Dyaus）之子；他駕馭著哈里特（Harits），是黎明之子，若非其子，至少是其寵愛者。此外，在希臘神話中，厄洛斯也有眾多父親與眾多母親；薩芙（Sappho）所賦予他的一對雙親——天與地——與他的吠陀式雙親特尤斯與伊妲（Ida）完全相同。
6 參見《馬克西慕斯‧泰魯斯》第二十四篇。希臘文原文見普雷勒《希臘神話學》第238頁。

庫爾提烏斯教授（Curtius）在《希臘詞源學綱要》（*Grundziige der Griechischen Etymologie*, 第 114 頁）中針對 épwc 與 arvat 共同起源所提出的異議值得仔細關注。「我們如何能將『épwe』與 époc、tpapat、épaw、éparde、éparecvoc 及其他年代久遠、甚至荷馬時代的詞彙區分開來呢？」他說，§ 「這些詞彙不可能源於『épwe』這個名稱，即使我們假設它們源於同一根詞 ar（其含義我們必須指定為行走、奔跑、努力），époc 將意味著努力或欲望，而要證明同源詞『épwe』源於此根詞便會有困難。」142 比較神話學。

印度卻非希臘；雖然我們可以追蹤希臘詞彙的根芽與根源，以及「馬」或「太陽馬」的希臘含義——在梵文中被賦予 arvat 之名——但我們不應因此就混淆兩者。柯蒂烏斯教授隨後對 Charis 的詞源提出相同的異議：「那麼我們應當如何處理 χάρις、χαρίζω、χαρίεις 呢？」關於 Charis，我可以引述我在《講座第二輯》第 368 頁所提供的解釋，希望我已經證明了 Charis 不能如柯蒂烏斯教授所提議的那樣，被置於與 Actaē 或 Sô 相同的神祇範疇中；而古代雅利安根詞的某些衍生詞呈現神話特徵，而其他詞則保留類似的通俗含義，這並非不合理之事。從根詞 dyu（閃耀）出發，我們有特尤斯（Dyaus）與宙斯（Zeús）；但我們在梵文中也有 diva 與 dina（日）、在希臘文中有 ἠῶ（在正午）、ἥλιος（光明）。從根詞 vas 或 ush（發光、燃燒）出發，我們有厄俄斯（Ēōs）、維斯塔（Vesta）、烏莎絲（Ushas）、厄俄斯（Eos）、歐若拉（Aurora）；但同樣地，梵文有 usra（早晨）、ushna（熾熱）；拉丁文有 urere（燃燒）、aurum（金）；希臘文有 αἴθω（燃燒）、αὖρος（乾燥）。除非我們設想根詞在產生單一神話之名後便陷入瞬時不孕，或希臘神話之名只能源自該語言中實際使用的根詞，否則我們在厄羅斯（Eros）與夏瑞忒（Charis）身上所觀察到的現象，乃是語言與神話增長的自然而幾乎是必然的結果——這正是我們現今對其的理解方式。希臘學者曾問道：「我們如何能將 χάρις 與 χαίρω 相分離，或如何能將ἐρέννυσις 與『Faseoey』相分離呢？」然而很少有人質疑昆恩對「巴厄斯」與「羅亞斯」詞源的解說，儘管對於這兩位神祇究竟是如何成為其今日模樣的確切過程，意見或有分歧。但另一方面，我必須強烈抗議已經被歸咎於我的觀點——即希臘人在任何方式上都意識到 arvat 與 harit 在印度已經採用的次級或習語含義。在印度，arvat（馬蹄行進）與 harit（光明）確實成為了公認的馬之名稱。由於 arvat 也被應用於太陽，這位天上的疾行者，因此太陽為馬的觀念幾乎是不可避免的，對於使用梵文的詩人來說毫無費力。希臘語中全然不同。在希臘語中，ἵππος（馬）決非曾如任何廣義的方式被用於太陽之名；遠在宙斯（Zeús）被用於閃耀的天空之前，ἀρή（競速）與 ἱέναι（匆急前進）的觀念早已相應而生。但除非我們準備將厄羅斯——「最古老的希臘諸神」——視為純粹的抽象概念，實際上視為某種丘比特，否則我認為、且依然認為，我們必須在最早崇拜厄羅斯的信徒中，即便在希臘土地上，也要承認對古代雅利安神話的模糊回憶——在該神話中，相同於厄羅斯的詞彙曾被應用於太陽，特別是朝陽。其餘的一切都簡單易明。根詞 ar 無疑具有疾行或匆促的含義，因此可能既產生了表達快速運動的名稱，也產生了表達強烈欲望的名稱。然而，並非每一株從此等種籽長出的枝條，在移植至不同的土壤時都能活存。厄羅斯本可如 arvat 在印度那樣成為希臘語中的馬之名稱，但它並未如此；arvat 或其他類似衍生詞如 artha，本可在梵文中如在希臘文中那樣表達欲望，但這也並未發生。為何某些詞彙消亡，而其他詞彙存活；為何某些詞義變得突出以致吸收所有其他含義，我們無從解釋。我們必須如其現狀接受語言的運作，在梳理這複雜的紋理時，我們不應期待發現一條連續不斷的線索，而應當滿足於能分離出斷裂的線端，並將其並列排放成某種可理解的秩序即可。希臘神話並非源借自吠陀神話，正如希臘詞彙並非取自梵文辭典。一旦這一點被理解並普遍承認，若此處彼處將吠陀神祇或梵文詞彙稱為「原型」，就不應引起反感。我知道這個表達並非恰當準確，只能以幾乎每個人都知道其含義為藉口來為之辯護。希臘的夏瑞忒（Charites）確實不是吠陀的哈麗塔斯（Haritas）的單純變化形式，希臘的厄羅斯也不是吠陀的阿爾瓦特（Arvat）的單純變化形式。在希臘厄羅斯或夏瑞忒中沒有對馬的特性的回憶，正如從純粹希臘的觀點來看，在『Σάρ-αμι＝薩拉彌』或『Ἑρμ-εῖαι＝薩拉梅亞』中無法發現任何犬的特性的蹤跡。阿爾瓦特與厄羅斯乃是從一個共同的中心思想出發的半徑，而吠陀半徑的角度比希臘半徑的角度更銳利。這就是所有可能意指的內涵，我相信這就是我言論被我大多數讀者所理解的意義。
屬於赫拉斯，柏拉圖在那裡向我們講述了厄洛斯（Eros）是什麼，索福克勒斯唱過他的「願厄洛斯保佑，願厄洛斯不曾在諸神中失去其力量，願厄洛斯以黃金般的光彩統治青年的心靈，願厄洛斯主宰人間的事務」。

如果黑格爾稱希臘語和梵語共同起源的發現為「新世界的發現」，那麼同樣可以說，希臘語和梵語神話共同起源的發現也是如此。這一發現已經完成，比較神話學將很快上升到與比較語言學相同的重要地位。我在此僅闡釋了少數幾則神話，但它們都屬於同一個小的循環，還可以增添許多更多的名字。對於這種語言地質學感興趣的人，我可以介紹他們參考由我的博學朋友柯恩博士（Dr. Kuhn）在柏林發行的《比較語言學雜誌》（Journal of Comparative Philology），他在其期刊中恰當地將比較神話學認可為比較語言學的整體部分，並且他本人在吠陀的傳統與其他雅利安民族的神話名稱之間發現了一些最引人注目的平行現象。那些「半人馬（Hippokentaurs）和奇美拉（Chimera）、戈耳貢（Gorgons）和珀伽索斯（Pevasos），以及其他怪物般的生物」，顯然已經被妥善說明；儘管我在此與……有不同看法。 6) 《安提戈涅》（Antigone），迪因多夫版，牛津 1859 年，第 781 行。

關於庫恩博士在若干要點上的看法，我與他存在分歧，特別是在諸神的基本性質問題上。他像那位令人哀悼的《希臘神話體系》作者勞埃爾一樣，似乎將諸神的性質過度侷限於雲彩、風暴和雷鳴等飄忽無常的現象，而我相信諸神的原始觀念幾乎總是與太陽相關的。儘管如此，我們仍有許多可以從兩者身上學習之處。誠然，還有許多工作有待完成，即使藉助《吠陀》的幫助，整個希臘神話也永遠無法被完全破譯和翻譯。但是，這是否可作為一種反對意見而被提出呢？有許多希臘詞彙，即使借助梵語也無法找到令人滿意的詞源，我們是否因此就應該說整個希臘語言沒有詞源的組織性呢？如果我們在希臘詞彙的一小部分形成中發現了一個有理性的原則，我們就有理由推斷，在部分顯現的同一原則也統治著整體的有機發展。雖然我們無法解釋所有詞彙的詞源起源，我們絕不應該說語言沒有詞源的起源，或者說詞源學「涉及一個從未出現過的過去」。我完全承認後來的希臘人，如荷馬和赫西俄德，對其神話的起源和意蘊渾然無知，但他們對其詞彙的起源和意蘊同樣無知。因此，適用於詞源學的東西也同樣適用於神話學。比較語言學已證明語言中沒有任何不規則之處，曾經被視為不規則的屈折變化和動詞變位現已被認可為語法形成中最規則和最原始的層位。我們希望，對神話學也可作同樣的證明。

可以在神話學中實現，而不是像以往那樣從「人類心智的衰弱與語言的貧乏」(ab ingenii humani imbecillitate et a dictionis egestate) 推導，反之將獲得其更真實的解答，「源於人類心智的智慧與語言的豐富」(ab ingenii humani sapientia et a dictionis abundantia)。神話學不過是一種方言，一種古代的語言形式。神話學雖然主要關涉自然，而在此又多半涉及那些帶有秩序、力量與智慧特徵的顯現，但卻適用於萬物。沒有任何事物被排除於神話表達之外；無論是倫理學還是哲學，無論是歷史還是宗教，都未能逃脫那位古代女預言家的魔力。然而神話學既非哲學，也非歷史，既非宗教，亦非倫理學。若採用經院哲學的用語，它是一種性質 (quale)，而非本質 (quid)——某種形式上的東西，而非實質上的東西——且如同詩歌、雕刻與繪畫一樣，適用於古代世界所能欣賞或崇拜的幾乎一切事物。
一八九三年四月六日。

我們很少有機會拿起一本八百多頁、印刷精密、充滿注釋和引文的德文書籍，而又捨不得放下它，直到把整本書讀完為止。然而，這正是我們對韋爾克教授的《希臘神話學》所經歷的狀況，也是許多讀者將會經歷的狀況——只要他們能夠以真實而人性的興趣進入古希臘人的生活、思想和情感，尤其是進入他們宗教的精神、他們的崇拜儀式和神聖傳統。對於那些需要關於作者的初步資訊的人，我們首先可以說的是，韋爾克是一位非常年邁的老人，一位幾乎已屬於往日時代的人物，是那英雄般的德國學術時代少數僅存的人物之一。現在的一代學者——一個本身不值得輕視的人種——仰望他如同希臘人仰望涅斯托爾一般。他年輕時曾認識老沃斯，荷馬的譯者，當時韋爾克正在以理性神話學對抗克魯策的象徵學派進行激烈的論戰。他是佐埃加的友人。他會說 ¹ 《希臘神靈論》，F. G. 韋爾克著，第一冊，哥廷根，1837 年。

布特曼（Buttmann）的弟子，作為一位感受過其教學影響的學者，他亦視自己與奧特弗里德‧穆勒──多利安的穆勒──在學術淵源上的關聯，雖然後者以獨立、有時過度獨立的精神推行了其導師的觀點。韋爾克爾多年以來講授並著述神話學，他欣然發現，許多他最初在講座中提出的觀點──那些向任何願意聆聽者開放的講座──早在其著作出版之前，已經流行開來，成為了某種公共財產。他不是一個好張揚優先權的人；如果他對這個主題有所著墨，也不過是為了自衛。他希望提醒讀者，如果他以發現者的熱忱提出某些觀點，如果他針對一切可能的異議而竭力為之辯護，那是因為他多年來已習慣於如此做，而且在他最初系統地闡述並在講座中詳加說明時，這樣做乃屬必要。韋爾克爾的《神話學》已被期待多年，在其問世之前早已引發諸多討論。作者說：「我為此深感遺憾，且此非我過錯，這般殷切的期待業已被提出。」然而，倘若德國各教授的期待誠為盛大，他們將承認其期待並未落空，年輕時代的韋爾克爾所作的承諾已為今日的耆老所踐行。

《希臘眾神之學》（*die Griechische Gotterlehre*）是該書的書名，雖然它能最迅速地引領讀者前行，在每一頁都激發好奇心，在每一章都開啟關於**希臘神話**的新視野，然而這卻是一部需要多次閱讀的著作。除了某些尚未臻於完善的章節外，它可以為了消遣而閱讀；但它值得被深入研究、反覆思考、審視和批評，唯有如此才能發現其真正的價值。近年來已有許多關於神話學的著作出版。普雷勒爾、革哈德、謝林、莫里相繼問世。普雷勒爾的《希臘神話學》是一部有用且嚴謹的摘要。革哈德的《希臘神話學》是一座知識寶庫，但有時更像是一座迷宮，堆滿了神話學的知識。至於謝林在其遺著中出版的《神話學哲學》，我們幾乎不敢貿然置評。然而，儘管對其偉大名聲懷有應有的尊重，儘管對其在神話學課題上的某些深邃思想懷有真摯的讚賞，更重要的是，儘管充分承認他在指出神話思想與語言在最廣泛意義上的必然性方面比任何人都更為強烈地指點出這一點的功績，但作為評論家，我們必須說，他的事實與理論悖於一切健全學術的規則，他的語言如此繁雜含糊，實不足以稱道於我們所處的這個時代。對於那些瞭解謝林的思想在本世紀初曾對德國施予多麼有力而重要影響的人來說，說出這些話實非易事。然而，儘管我們閱讀他的遺著時無不感到悲愴，並強烈感受到人類知識之必朽性，但在必須提及他們之際，我們不能不表達我們的確信：雖然這些著作因其作者的身份而令人感到興趣，但在其他各個方面都令人失望。莫里的《宗教史》
『古代希臘的宗教』如同該位勤勉作家的所有著作一般，文筆清晰而優雅。它並未聲稱在各個論述主題上，對既有知識增添許多獨立研究的成果。因此，這位才華洋溢的作者得以避免批評，而只換得所有閱讀其精心編纂之手冊讀者的感謝。
